name: Manananggal
description: The Manananggal is a fearsome creature in Philippine folklore, a self-segmenting vampire-like being of terrifying power. It is depicted as an older, often female, human capable of severing its upper torso from its lower body, sprouting bat-like wings, and flying into the night to prey on unsuspecting victims. Its favorite targets are pregnant women, whose unborn children's hearts it consumes, and sleeping individuals, whose blood it sucks with its long, proboscis-like tongue. The lower half of the body remains standing upright, usually hidden in a thicket or dark area, making it vulnerable. Salt, garlic, and blessed oil are believed to repel the Manananggal. The creature is often associated with rural areas and is said to be more active during the full moon. Its name derives from the Tagalog word "tanggal," which means "to separate" or "to remove."
subtitle: Self-segmenting vampire of Philippine folklore
appearance: A Manananggal typically appears as an attractive human female during the day. At night, she separates her upper torso from her lower body, sprouting large, bat-like wings. Her entrails dangle from her open torso, and she uses a long, thin tongue to suck blood and extract fetuses.
habitat: Rural areas, particularly in the Visayas region of the Philippines. They are said to inhabit dark forests, abandoned houses, and areas near populated villages.
behavior: Manananggals are nocturnal creatures, active only at night. They are cunning and manipulative, often disguising themselves as ordinary humans to lure victims. They are known to be aggressive and territorial, defending their hunting grounds fiercely.
weaknesses: Destroying the lower half of the body

keyMyths.title: The Pregnant Woman's Nightmare
keyMyths.description: A pregnant woman living in a remote village began experiencing strange occurrences at night. She would hear flapping sounds near her window and feel a cold presence. Eventually, she discovered the truth: a Manananggal was targeting her unborn child. She protected herself and her baby with salt and garlic, eventually driving the creature away. The story serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers lurking in the night.

keyMyths.title: The Separated Torso
keyMyths.description: A young man fell in love with a beautiful woman, unaware of her true nature. One night, he followed her into the forest and witnessed her separating her torso from her lower body. Horrified, he sprinkled salt on the exposed lower half, preventing her from rejoining. As dawn approached, the Manananggal perished, unable to return to her human form. This story highlights the creature's vulnerability and the importance of knowing one's partner.

keyMyths.title: The Village Protector
keyMyths.description: A brave warrior learned about the existence of a Manananggal terrorizing his village. He tracked the creature to its lair and discovered its separated lower body. He destroyed it, ensuring the creature could never return. The village rejoiced, hailing him as a hero who saved them from the dreaded Manananggal. This tale emphasizes courage and the fight against evil.

sources.author: Maximo Ramos
sources.work: The Creatures of Philippine Lower Mythology
sources.description: A comprehensive study of various mythical creatures in Philippine folklore, including detailed information about the Manananggal's origins and characteristics.
sources.text: A comprehensive study of various mythical creatures in Philippine folklore, including detailed information about the Manananggal's origins and characteristics.

sources.author: Damiana L. Eugenio
sources.description: A collection of Philippine folk tales and myths, featuring numerous stories about the Manananggal and its encounters with humans.
sources.text: A collection of Philippine folk tales and myths, featuring numerous stories about the Manananggal and its encounters with humans.

sources.author: Herminia Q. Meñez
sources.description: An exploration of how folklore, including stories of creatures like the Manananggal, functions as a means of communication and cultural transmission within Filipino communities.
